title: IT Project Governance
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a governance framework for all technology projects that includes defined roles and responsibilities, approval gates, and oversight mechanisms. Major technology projects shall be subject to independent risk assessment and board or senior management approval prior to commencement.
translation: You need a formal governance framework for IT projects with clear roles, stage-gate approvals, and oversight. Major projects must undergo an independent risk assessment and get board or senior management approval before they start.
evidence: Project approval records with stage-gate sign-offs
Independent risk assessment reports for major projects
Board or senior management approval records

artifacts.name: IT Project Governance Framework
artifacts.description: Framework defining project governance structures, roles, and approval gates

artifacts.name: Project Approval Records
artifacts.description: Records of project approvals and stage-gate sign-offs

artifacts.name: Project Risk Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Independent risk assessment reports for major technology projects

title: Project Risk Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct risk assessments for all technology projects to identify potential risks to security, availability, and data integrity. The assessment shall consider interdependencies with existing systems and the potential impact on customers and business operations.
translation: Every IT project must have a risk assessment covering security, availability, and data integrity. You also need to consider how the project interacts with existing systems and what impact it could have on customers and day-to-day operations.
evidence: Project risk assessment template and methodology
Risk mitigation plans for identified project risks

artifacts.name: Project Risk Assessment Methodology
artifacts.description: Standard methodology and template for assessing technology project risks

artifacts.name: Project Risk Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Completed risk assessments for technology projects

artifacts.name: Interdependency Analysis
artifacts.description: Analysis of project interdependencies with existing systems

title: Post-Implementation Review
verbatim: Financial institutions should conduct post-implementation reviews for all significant technology projects to assess whether project objectives were achieved, identify lessons learned, and evaluate the effectiveness of risk mitigation measures. Reviews should be completed within three months of project go-live.
translation: After major IT projects go live, you should conduct a formal review within three months to check if objectives were met, capture lessons learned, and evaluate whether risk controls are working as intended.

artifacts.name: Post-Implementation Review Report
artifacts.description: Review report assessing project outcomes and lessons learned

artifacts.name: Lessons Learned Register
artifacts.description: Register of lessons learned from completed technology projects

artifacts.name: Risk Control Effectiveness Assessment
artifacts.description: Assessment of whether implemented risk controls are operating effectively

title: SDLC Framework
verbatim: Financial institutions shall adopt a formal software development life cycle (SDLC) framework that integrates security at every phase. The framework shall include requirements gathering, design, development, testing, deployment, and maintenance phases with defined security checkpoints and quality gates.
translation: You must use a formal SDLC framework where security is built into every phase from requirements through to maintenance. Each phase must have defined security checkpoints and quality gates that must be passed before proceeding.
evidence: Quality gate criteria and sign-off records
Evidence of framework adherence in recent projects

artifacts.name: SDLC Framework Document
artifacts.description: Formal SDLC framework with security integrated at every phase

artifacts.name: Quality Gate Records
artifacts.description: Records of quality gate assessments and sign-offs for projects

artifacts.name: Security Checkpoint Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of security reviews conducted at each SDLC phase

title: Requirements Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall ensure that security and compliance requirements are explicitly defined and documented during the requirements phase of system development. Requirements shall be traceable throughout the development lifecycle and validated prior to deployment.
translation: Security and compliance requirements must be clearly documented at the start of every development project. These requirements must be traceable through design, build, and test so you can prove each one was implemented and validated before go-live.

artifacts.name: Requirements Management Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for capturing, tracing, and validating security requirements

artifacts.name: Requirements Traceability Matrix
artifacts.description: Matrix tracing security requirements through development phases to validation

artifacts.name: Requirements Validation Records
artifacts.description: Records demonstrating validation of security requirements prior to deployment

title: Secure Development Standards
verbatim: Financial institutions shall adopt and enforce secure coding standards based on recognised industry practices such as OWASP. Developers shall be trained on secure coding practices and compliance with these standards shall be verified through code reviews and automated scanning tools.
translation: You must have secure coding standards based on industry best practices like OWASP. All developers must be trained on them, and you must verify compliance through both manual code reviews and automated scanning tools.
evidence: Developer training records on secure coding
Code review records referencing coding standards
Automated scanning tool configuration and results

artifacts.name: Secure Coding Standards
artifacts.description: Coding standards based on OWASP and industry best practices

artifacts.name: Developer Training Records
artifacts.description: Records of developer training on secure coding practices

artifacts.name: Code Review Logs
artifacts.description: Logs of code reviews conducted against secure coding standards

title: Code Review & Static Analysis
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement mandatory code review processes and static application security testing (SAST) for all application code prior to deployment to production. Critical and high-severity findings shall be remediated before release, and exceptions shall require formal risk acceptance.
translation: All code must go through both peer code reviews and automated static analysis (SAST) before it reaches production. Any critical or high-severity issues found must be fixed before release. If you cannot fix them, you need formal documented risk acceptance.
evidence: SAST tool configuration and scan results
Remediation records for critical and high findings

artifacts.name: Code Review Procedure
artifacts.description: Mandatory procedure for peer code reviews and SAST scanning

artifacts.name: SAST Scan Results
artifacts.description: Static analysis scan results for application releases

artifacts.name: Remediation Records
artifacts.description: Records of remediation of critical and high findings before release

title: Testing Standards
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish comprehensive testing standards that include functional testing, integration testing, performance testing, and security testing. Test plans shall be documented and test results shall be formally reviewed and approved prior to production deployment.
translation: You must have formal testing standards covering functional, integration, performance, and security testing. Test plans must be documented upfront, and test results must be reviewed and approved before anything goes to production.
evidence: Test plan templates and completed test plans
Test execution results and defect reports

artifacts.name: Testing Standards Document
artifacts.description: Comprehensive standards for functional, integration, performance, and security testing

artifacts.name: Test Plans and Results
artifacts.description: Documented test plans and formally reviewed test execution results

artifacts.name: Test Summary Reports
artifacts.description: Summary reports of testing outcomes and approval decisions

title: UAT & Acceptance
verbatim: Financial institutions shall ensure that user acceptance testing (UAT) is conducted by business users in an environment that mirrors production. UAT results shall be formally documented and signed off by business owners prior to production deployment. UAT environments shall not contain production data unless appropriately masked.
translation: Business users must conduct UAT in an environment that closely resembles production. Results must be formally documented and signed off by business owners before go-live. If production data is used in UAT, it must be properly masked or anonymised.
evidence: UAT environment specification and comparison to production
UAT sign-off records from business owners
Data masking procedures for UAT environments

artifacts.name: UAT Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure defining UAT requirements, environment standards, and sign-off process

artifacts.name: UAT Sign-off Records
artifacts.description: Formal sign-off records from business owners for UAT completion

artifacts.name: Data Masking Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for masking production data used in non-production environments

title: Change Management Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a formal change management policy that governs all changes to production systems, including applications, infrastructure, and configurations. All changes shall be assessed for risk, tested, approved by authorised personnel, and documented in a change log.
translation: Every change to production systems — applications, infrastructure, or configurations — must go through a formal change management process. Each change needs a risk assessment, testing, authorised approval, and must be logged in a change register.
evidence: Change advisory board terms of reference
Sample change records with risk assessment and approvals

artifacts.name: Change Management Policy
artifacts.description: Formal policy governing all changes to production systems

artifacts.name: Change Advisory Board Terms of Reference
artifacts.description: Terms of reference for the change advisory board

artifacts.name: Change Log
artifacts.description: Complete log of all production changes with risk assessments and approvals

title: Emergency Change Procedures
verbatim: Financial institutions shall define procedures for emergency changes that bypass normal change management processes. Emergency changes shall be subject to retrospective review and approval within a defined timeframe. The use of emergency change procedures shall be monitored and reported to management.
translation: You need specific procedures for handling urgent changes that cannot wait for the normal process. Emergency changes must be retrospectively reviewed and approved within a set timeframe, and management must receive reports on how often emergency changes are used.
evidence: Emergency change records with retrospective approvals
Management reports on emergency change frequency

artifacts.name: Emergency Change Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for handling emergency changes including retrospective review requirements

artifacts.name: Emergency Change Records
artifacts.description: Records of all emergency changes with retrospective review and approval

artifacts.name: Emergency Change Usage Report
artifacts.description: Report on frequency and nature of emergency changes for management review

title: Release Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a release management process that ensures controlled and orderly deployment of changes to production environments. Release plans shall include rollback procedures, deployment verification steps, and defined success criteria.
translation: You must have a structured release management process for deploying changes to production. Every release plan needs rollback procedures in case something goes wrong, verification steps to confirm the deployment worked, and clear criteria for what constitutes a successful release.
evidence: Sample release plans with rollback procedures

artifacts.name: Release Management Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for controlled deployment of changes to production environments

artifacts.name: Release Plans
artifacts.description: Release plans including rollback procedures and success criteria

artifacts.name: Deployment Verification Records
artifacts.description: Records of post-deployment verification checks

title: Environment Separation
verbatim: Financial institutions shall maintain strict separation between development, testing, staging, and production environments. Access to production environments shall be restricted and controlled. Migration of code between environments shall follow a defined and auditable process.
translation: Development, testing, staging, and production must be clearly separated environments. Access to production must be tightly restricted, and moving code between environments must follow a formal, auditable process with proper controls.
evidence: Code migration procedures and audit trail

artifacts.name: Environment Separation Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining separation requirements between development, test, and production environments

artifacts.name: Environment Architecture Diagram
artifacts.description: Architecture diagram showing separation between environments

artifacts.name: Code Migration Audit Trail
artifacts.description: Audit trail of code migrations between environments

title: Source Code Protection
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement adequate controls to protect source code from unauthorised access, modification, and loss. Source code repositories shall be access-controlled and changes shall be tracked through version control systems. Source code shall not be stored in production environments.
translation: Source code must be protected from unauthorised access and changes. Use version-controlled repositories with proper access controls, track all changes, and ensure source code is never stored directly on production servers.
evidence: Evidence that source code is not in production environments

artifacts.name: Source Code Protection Policy
artifacts.description: Policy for protecting source code from unauthorised access, modification, and loss

artifacts.name: Repository Access Controls
artifacts.description: Configuration evidence showing access controls on source code repositories

artifacts.name: Version Control Logs
artifacts.description: Logs from version control system showing change tracking and audit trail

title: Configuration Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish configuration management processes to maintain an accurate and complete inventory of all technology assets, their configurations, and interdependencies. Configuration baselines shall be established and deviations shall be detected, assessed, and remediated.
translation: You must manage configurations formally — keeping an accurate inventory of all technology assets and their settings. Establish approved configuration baselines and have processes to detect, assess, and fix any deviations from those baselines.

artifacts.name: Configuration Management Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for maintaining configuration baselines and managing changes

artifacts.name: Configuration Management Database
artifacts.description: Inventory of technology assets with their configurations and interdependencies

artifacts.name: Configuration Compliance Report
artifacts.description: Report on configuration baseline compliance and deviation remediation

title: Capacity Planning
verbatim: Financial institutions should establish capacity planning processes to ensure that technology resources are sufficient to meet current and projected business requirements. Capacity thresholds should be defined and monitored, with timely scaling or procurement actions taken when thresholds are approached.
translation: You should plan technology capacity to ensure systems can handle current and future demand. Define capacity thresholds, monitor them continuously, and take action to scale up or procure more resources before limits are reached.

artifacts.name: Capacity Planning Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for planning, monitoring, and managing technology capacity

artifacts.name: Capacity Monitoring Reports
artifacts.description: Regular reports on capacity utilisation against defined thresholds

artifacts.name: Capacity Scaling Records
artifacts.description: Records of capacity scaling actions taken in response to threshold alerts

title: Patch Management Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a formal patch management policy that defines the processes for identifying, evaluating, testing, and deploying patches across all technology assets. The policy shall define patch classification criteria and maximum timeframes for deployment based on severity.
translation: You must have a written patch management policy covering how patches are identified, evaluated, tested, and deployed. The policy must classify patches by severity and set maximum timeframes for each — for example, critical patches within 14 days, high within 30 days.
evidence: Patch deployment records and compliance metrics

artifacts.name: Patch Management Policy
artifacts.description: Formal policy defining patch identification, evaluation, testing, and deployment processes

artifacts.name: Patch Testing Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for testing patches before deployment to production

artifacts.name: Patch Compliance Report
artifacts.description: Report on patch deployment compliance against defined timelines

title: Patch Deployment Timeline
verbatim: Financial institutions shall deploy critical security patches within fourteen days of release and high-severity patches within thirty days. Where immediate patching is not feasible, compensating controls shall be implemented and documented, with formal risk acceptance by the appropriate authority.
translation: Critical patches must be applied within 14 days and high-severity patches within 30 days. If you truly cannot patch in time, you must implement compensating controls, document them, and get formal risk acceptance from the appropriate authority level.
evidence: Compensating control documentation for delayed patches
Risk acceptance records for patch exceptions

artifacts.name: Patch Deployment Timeline Standards
artifacts.description: Defined timelines for patch deployment by severity level

artifacts.name: Patch Deployment Tracking Log
artifacts.description: Tracking log of all patch deployments against timeline requirements

artifacts.name: Patch Exception Records
artifacts.description: Documented compensating controls and risk acceptance for delayed patches

title: End-of-Life Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall maintain an inventory of all technology assets approaching or past end-of-life and end-of-support status. A migration or replacement plan shall be established for all end-of-life systems. Where end-of-life systems remain in use, compensating controls and formal risk acceptance shall be documented.
translation: You must track all systems approaching or past end-of-life or end-of-support dates. Each one needs a migration or replacement plan. If an EOL system cannot be replaced yet, you need documented compensating controls and formal risk acceptance.
evidence: Migration or replacement plans for EOL systems
Compensating control documentation for legacy systems
Risk acceptance records for continued EOL system use

artifacts.name: End-of-Life Asset Register
artifacts.description: Inventory of all assets approaching or past end-of-life or end-of-support

artifacts.name: EOL Migration Plans
artifacts.description: Migration or replacement plans for end-of-life systems

artifacts.name: EOL Risk Acceptance Records
artifacts.description: Formal risk acceptance and compensating controls for systems remaining past EOL

title: Cryptographic Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a cryptographic policy that defines the approved cryptographic algorithms, key lengths, and protocols for protecting data at rest and in transit. The policy shall be aligned with recognised international standards and reviewed at least annually to address cryptographic advancements and vulnerabilities.
translation: You need a written cryptographic policy specifying which algorithms, key lengths, and protocols are approved for protecting data at rest and in transit. Align it with international standards (like NIST) and review it yearly to stay current with evolving threats.
evidence: Approved algorithm and key length specifications

artifacts.name: Cryptographic Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining approved algorithms, key lengths, and protocols

artifacts.name: Standards Alignment Mapping
artifacts.description: Mapping of cryptographic policy to international standards (NIST, ISO)

artifacts.name: Annual Review Records
artifacts.description: Records of annual cryptographic policy review and updates

title: Key Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement a key management framework that governs the entire lifecycle of cryptographic keys, including generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction. Keys shall be protected using hardware security modules or equivalent mechanisms for critical systems.
translation: Manage cryptographic keys through their full lifecycle — generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction. For critical systems, keys must be stored in hardware security modules (HSMs) or similarly secure mechanisms.

artifacts.name: Key Management Framework
artifacts.description: Framework governing the full lifecycle of cryptographic keys

artifacts.name: HSM Configuration Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of HSM deployment and configuration for critical system keys

artifacts.name: Key Lifecycle Records
artifacts.description: Records of key generation, rotation, revocation, and destruction activities

title: Encryption Standards
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement encryption for all sensitive data at rest and in transit using approved cryptographic algorithms with a minimum key length of 256 bits for symmetric encryption and 2048 bits for asymmetric encryption. Deprecated or weak algorithms shall be identified and replaced.
translation: All sensitive data must be encrypted both at rest and in transit. Use AES-256 or equivalent for symmetric encryption and at least RSA-2048 for asymmetric. Identify and replace any deprecated or weak algorithms still in use.
evidence: Inventory of encryption algorithms in use

artifacts.name: Encryption Standards
artifacts.description: Standards specifying minimum encryption requirements for data at rest and in transit

artifacts.name: Encryption Implementation Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of encryption deployment across systems and data stores

artifacts.name: Cryptographic Algorithm Inventory
artifacts.description: Inventory of all cryptographic algorithms in use with assessment of adequacy

title: Certificate Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish processes for managing digital certificates, including issuance, renewal, revocation, and monitoring of certificate expiry. Certificates shall be obtained from trusted certificate authorities and certificate transparency shall be monitored to detect unauthorised issuance.
translation: You must manage digital certificates through their lifecycle — issue, renew, revoke, and monitor them for expiry. Use trusted certificate authorities and monitor certificate transparency logs to detect any unauthorised certificates issued for your domains.

artifacts.name: Certificate Management Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for managing the lifecycle of digital certificates

artifacts.name: Certificate Inventory
artifacts.description: Inventory of all digital certificates with issuance and expiry details

artifacts.name: Certificate Monitoring Logs
artifacts.description: Logs from certificate transparency monitoring and expiry alerts

title: Data Centre Physical Security
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement physical security controls for data centres that include perimeter security, multi-layer access controls, CCTV surveillance with a minimum retention of ninety days, and visitor management procedures. Physical access shall be restricted to authorised personnel only.
translation: Data centres must have multiple layers of physical security: perimeter fencing, multi-layer access controls (badges, biometrics), CCTV with at least 90 days of footage retention, and formal visitor management. Only authorised personnel may enter.
evidence: CCTV coverage maps and retention configuration

artifacts.name: Data Centre Physical Security Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining physical security requirements for data centre facilities

artifacts.name: CCTV Configuration Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of CCTV coverage and 90-day retention configuration

artifacts.name: Visitor Management Logs
artifacts.description: Logs of all visitor access to data centre facilities

title: Environmental Controls
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement environmental controls in data centres including temperature and humidity monitoring, water leak detection, and air quality management. Environmental conditions shall be continuously monitored with automated alerting when thresholds are breached.
translation: Data centres need environmental controls for temperature, humidity, water leaks, and air quality. These must be monitored continuously with automated alerts when any parameter goes outside acceptable thresholds.

artifacts.name: Environmental Control Procedures
artifacts.description: Procedures for monitoring and managing data centre environmental conditions

artifacts.name: Environmental Monitoring Configuration
artifacts.description: Configuration of environmental monitoring systems and alert thresholds

artifacts.name: Environmental Monitoring Logs
artifacts.description: Continuous monitoring logs for temperature, humidity, and other environmental parameters

title: Power & Redundancy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall ensure that data centres are equipped with redundant power supplies, uninterruptible power supply (UPS) systems, and backup generators. Power redundancy shall be designed to sustain operations during power outages with a minimum runtime of seventy-two hours for backup generators.
translation: Data centres must have redundant power including UPS systems and backup generators. Generators must be able to sustain operations for at least 72 hours during a power outage. Regular testing of power failover must be conducted.
evidence: Generator capacity and fuel supply records

artifacts.name: Power Redundancy Architecture
artifacts.description: Documentation of redundant power design including UPS and generator capacity

artifacts.name: Power Failover Test Records
artifacts.description: Records of regular power failover and generator testing

artifacts.name: Power Capacity Report
artifacts.description: Report on power capacity, redundancy levels, and generator fuel supply

title: Fire Suppression
verbatim: Financial institutions shall install and maintain fire detection and suppression systems in data centres that are appropriate for the protection of electronic equipment. Fire suppression systems shall be tested at least annually and staff shall be trained on emergency response procedures.
translation: Data centres need fire detection and suppression systems suitable for protecting electronic equipment (such as gas-based suppression). Systems must be tested yearly and staff must be trained on what to do in a fire emergency.

artifacts.name: Fire Suppression System Documentation
artifacts.description: Specifications and design of fire detection and suppression systems

artifacts.name: Fire System Test Records
artifacts.description: Annual testing records for fire detection and suppression systems

artifacts.name: Emergency Response Training Records
artifacts.description: Records of staff training on fire emergency response procedures

title: Data Centre Access Control
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement multi-factor physical access controls for data centre facilities, including biometric authentication for sensitive areas. Access rights shall be reviewed at least quarterly and immediately revoked upon termination of employment or change in role.
translation: Data centre access must use multi-factor physical authentication, with biometrics for sensitive areas. Access rights must be reviewed quarterly and immediately removed when someone leaves the organisation or changes role.
evidence: Access revocation records for terminations and role changes

artifacts.name: Data Centre Access Control Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for managing physical access to data centre facilities

artifacts.name: Access Control System Configuration
artifacts.description: Configuration of multi-factor physical access controls and biometric systems

artifacts.name: Access Review and Revocation Records
artifacts.description: Quarterly access review records and immediate revocation evidence

title: Service Level Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall define and monitor service level agreements for all critical technology services. SLAs shall specify availability targets, performance metrics, and incident response times. Service level performance shall be reported to senior management at least monthly.
translation: Define SLAs for all critical technology services covering availability, performance, and incident response times. Monitor them continuously and report performance to senior management at least monthly.
evidence: Monthly SLA performance reports to management
SLA breach records and remediation actions

artifacts.name: Service Level Agreements
artifacts.description: Documented SLAs for all critical technology services

artifacts.name: Monthly SLA Performance Reports
artifacts.description: Monthly reports on service level performance presented to management

artifacts.name: SLA Breach Records
artifacts.description: Records of SLA breaches and corrective actions taken

title: IT Service Continuity
verbatim: Financial institutions shall develop and maintain IT service continuity plans that ensure the continued availability of critical technology services during disruptions. Plans shall be aligned with business continuity management and shall address various disruption scenarios including natural disasters, cyber attacks, and technology failures.
translation: You must have IT service continuity plans for keeping critical systems running during disruptions. Plans should cover natural disasters, cyber attacks, and technology failures, and must align with broader business continuity planning.
evidence: Alignment mapping with business continuity plans

artifacts.name: IT Service Continuity Plan
artifacts.description: Plans ensuring continued availability of critical services during disruptions

artifacts.name: BCP Alignment Mapping
artifacts.description: Mapping showing alignment between IT continuity and business continuity plans

artifacts.name: Scenario Analysis Report
artifacts.description: Analysis of disruption scenarios and their impact on critical services

title: Disaster Recovery Planning
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish disaster recovery plans for all critical systems that define recovery procedures, roles and responsibilities, communication plans, and escalation procedures. DR plans shall be documented, maintained, and accessible to relevant personnel during an emergency.
translation: Every critical system needs a disaster recovery plan covering how to recover it, who is responsible, how to communicate during the crisis, and when to escalate. Plans must be kept up to date and accessible during emergencies — not locked away on the systems that failed.
evidence: Disaster recovery plan documents for critical systems
DR plan distribution and accessibility evidence

artifacts.name: Disaster Recovery Plans
artifacts.description: DR plans for all critical systems with recovery procedures and roles

artifacts.name: DR Roles and Responsibilities Matrix
artifacts.description: Matrix defining DR roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths

artifacts.name: DR Communication Plan
artifacts.description: Communication plan for use during disaster recovery activation

title: DR Testing
verbatim: Financial institutions shall test disaster recovery plans at least annually for all critical systems. Testing shall include full failover tests to the disaster recovery site and shall validate recovery time objectives and recovery point objectives. Test results shall be documented and deficiencies remediated.
translation: DR plans for critical systems must be tested at least once a year with full failover to the DR site. Tests must confirm that RTO and RPO targets are met. Document the results, identify any gaps, and fix them.
evidence: DR test schedule and completion records

artifacts.name: DR Test Results Report
artifacts.description: Report on disaster recovery testing including RTO/RPO validation

artifacts.name: Failover Test Records
artifacts.description: Records of full failover tests to the disaster recovery site

artifacts.name: DR Deficiency Remediation Plan
artifacts.description: Plan for remediating deficiencies identified during DR testing

title: Recovery Time Objectives
verbatim: Financial institutions shall define recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for all critical systems based on business impact analysis. RTOs and RPOs shall be approved by business owners and validated through regular disaster recovery testing.
translation: Define RTO and RPO targets for all critical systems based on how important they are to the business. Business owners must approve these targets, and regular DR testing must prove the targets can actually be met.
evidence: RTO and RPO definitions for critical systems
DR test results validating RTO/RPO compliance
Business impact analysis supporting RTO/RPO decisions

artifacts.name: RTO/RPO Definitions
artifacts.description: Documented RTO and RPO targets for all critical systems with business owner approval

artifacts.name: RTO/RPO Validation Records
artifacts.description: DR test records validating that RTO and RPO targets are achievable

artifacts.name: BIA-to-RTO Mapping
artifacts.description: Mapping between business impact analysis results and RTO/RPO targets

title: Business Impact Analysis
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct a business impact analysis (BIA) at least annually to identify critical business processes, their supporting technology services, and the potential impact of disruptions. The BIA shall inform recovery priorities, resource allocation, and continuity planning decisions.
translation: Conduct a BIA at least yearly to identify which business processes are critical, what technology services support them, and what happens if they go down. Use the BIA results to set recovery priorities and allocate continuity resources.
evidence: Annual BIA review and update records

artifacts.name: Business Impact Analysis Report
artifacts.description: Annual BIA identifying critical processes and their technology dependencies

artifacts.name: Technology Dependency Mapping
artifacts.description: Mapping of critical business processes to supporting technology services

artifacts.name: BIA Review Records
artifacts.description: Annual review and update records for the business impact analysis

title: Crisis Communication
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish crisis communication plans that define communication protocols, stakeholder notification procedures, and media management strategies during technology incidents and disruptions. Communication plans shall be tested as part of regular continuity exercises.
translation: You need a crisis communication plan that covers how to communicate during technology incidents — who gets notified, in what order, through which channels, and how to manage external communications and media. Test these plans during continuity exercises.
evidence: Stakeholder notification procedures and contact lists

artifacts.name: Crisis Communication Plan
artifacts.description: Plan defining communication protocols during technology incidents and disruptions

artifacts.name: Stakeholder Contact Lists
artifacts.description: Maintained contact lists for crisis notification of stakeholders

artifacts.name: Communication Plan Test Records
artifacts.description: Records of crisis communication testing during continuity exercises

title: Network Architecture
verbatim: Financial institutions shall design and maintain a secure network architecture that incorporates defence-in-depth principles. The architecture shall be documented, including all network zones, data flows, and interconnections. Architecture reviews shall be conducted at least annually and upon significant changes.
translation: Your network must be designed with defence-in-depth — multiple layers of security. Document the full architecture including all zones, data flows, and connections. Review the architecture at least yearly or when significant changes occur.
evidence: Network zone definitions and data flow diagrams

artifacts.name: Network Architecture Diagrams
artifacts.description: Complete network architecture diagrams including zones, data flows, and interconnections

artifacts.name: Network Architecture Standards
artifacts.description: Standards for secure network architecture based on defence-in-depth principles

artifacts.name: Annual Architecture Review Report
artifacts.description: Annual review report on network architecture adequacy and security

title: Network Segmentation
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement network segmentation to isolate critical systems, sensitive data environments, and different trust zones. Segmentation controls shall restrict lateral movement and shall be validated through regular penetration testing.
translation: Segment your network to isolate critical systems and sensitive data from less secure areas. The segmentation must prevent attackers from moving laterally across network zones, and it should be validated through regular penetration testing.

artifacts.name: Network Segmentation Design
artifacts.description: Documentation of network segmentation design and trust zone definitions

artifacts.name: Segmentation Configuration Evidence
artifacts.description: VLAN and firewall configurations implementing network segmentation

artifacts.name: Segmentation Validation Reports
artifacts.description: Penetration test reports validating effectiveness of network segmentation

title: Firewall Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement and maintain firewalls at all network boundaries and between security zones. Firewall rules shall follow the principle of least privilege, be reviewed at least semi-annually, and unused or overly permissive rules shall be identified and remediated.
translation: Deploy firewalls at all network boundaries and between security zones. Rules must follow least privilege, be reviewed at least every six months, and any unused or overly permissive rules must be cleaned up.
evidence: Remediation records for overly permissive rules

artifacts.name: Firewall Management Policy
artifacts.description: Policy governing firewall deployment, rule management, and review cycles

artifacts.name: Firewall Rule Review Records
artifacts.description: Semi-annual firewall rule review records with findings and actions

artifacts.name: Firewall Audit Report
artifacts.description: Audit report on firewall rule base identifying unused or permissive rules

title: Intrusion Detection & Prevention
verbatim: Financial institutions shall deploy intrusion detection and prevention systems (IDS/IPS) at critical network points to detect and block malicious activities. IDS/IPS signatures and rules shall be updated regularly, and alerts shall be monitored and investigated in a timely manner.
translation: Deploy IDS/IPS at critical network points to detect and block attacks. Keep signatures and rules updated, and ensure alerts are actively monitored and investigated promptly — not just collected and ignored.

artifacts.name: IDS/IPS Deployment Documentation
artifacts.description: Architecture and deployment documentation for IDS/IPS systems

artifacts.name: Signature Update Records
artifacts.description: Records of IDS/IPS signature and rule updates

artifacts.name: Alert Investigation Records
artifacts.description: Records of IDS/IPS alert monitoring, triage, and investigation

title: Wireless Security
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement security controls for all wireless networks, including WPA3 or equivalent encryption, network segmentation from the corporate network, rogue access point detection, and regular wireless security assessments. Guest wireless networks shall be isolated from internal networks.
translation: Wireless networks must use WPA3 or equivalent encryption and be segmented from the corporate network. Implement rogue access point detection, conduct regular wireless security assessments, and ensure guest Wi-Fi is completely isolated from internal systems.
evidence: Rogue AP detection system deployment evidence

artifacts.name: Wireless Security Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining security requirements for wireless network deployment

artifacts.name: Wireless Configuration Evidence
artifacts.description: Configuration evidence for wireless encryption, segmentation, and rogue AP detection

artifacts.name: Wireless Security Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Regular wireless security assessment and penetration test reports

title: Remote Access Security
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement secure remote access solutions that include multi-factor authentication, encrypted tunnels, and endpoint compliance checks. Remote access sessions shall be logged and monitored. Split tunnelling shall be prohibited for connections to the corporate network.
translation: Remote access must use MFA, encrypted VPN tunnels, and endpoint compliance checks (ensuring the device meets security standards before connecting). Log and monitor all remote sessions. Split tunnelling must be disabled to prevent bypassing corporate security controls.
evidence: Remote access monitoring and logging evidence

artifacts.name: Remote Access Security Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining security requirements for remote access to corporate resources

artifacts.name: Remote Access Configuration
artifacts.description: Configuration evidence for VPN, MFA, and endpoint compliance checks

artifacts.name: Remote Access Logs
artifacts.description: Logs of remote access sessions with monitoring and investigation records

title: DNS Security
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement DNS security controls including DNSSEC for authoritative domains, DNS query logging and monitoring, and protection against DNS-based attacks such as cache poisoning and tunnelling. DNS infrastructure shall be designed with redundancy and resilience.
translation: Implement DNS security measures including DNSSEC for your domains, DNS query logging, and defences against DNS attacks like cache poisoning and DNS tunnelling. Ensure DNS infrastructure is redundant and resilient.
evidence: DNS query logging and monitoring evidence

artifacts.name: DNS Security Standards
artifacts.description: Standards for DNS security including DNSSEC and attack prevention

artifacts.name: DNS Security Configuration
artifacts.description: Configuration evidence for DNSSEC, logging, and DNS attack prevention

artifacts.name: DNS Infrastructure Design
artifacts.description: DNS infrastructure design showing redundancy and resilience measures

title: Network Monitoring
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement comprehensive network monitoring to detect anomalous traffic patterns, unauthorised access attempts, and potential security incidents. Network traffic shall be analysed using flow data and deep packet inspection where appropriate. Monitoring shall operate on a twenty-four by seven basis.
translation: Monitor your network 24/7 to detect unusual traffic patterns, unauthorised access attempts, and security incidents. Use network flow analysis and deep packet inspection where appropriate to gain visibility into what is traversing the network.
evidence: Anomaly detection configuration and alert records

artifacts.name: Network Monitoring Architecture
artifacts.description: Architecture and tooling documentation for comprehensive network monitoring

artifacts.name: Network Monitoring Procedures
artifacts.description: Procedures for 24/7 network monitoring and anomaly investigation

artifacts.name: Network Monitoring Alerts
artifacts.description: Logs of network monitoring alerts and investigation outcomes

title: Backup Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a backup policy that defines backup scope, frequency, retention periods, and storage requirements for all critical systems and data. Backups shall be encrypted, stored in geographically separate locations, and protected from unauthorised access and ransomware attacks.
translation: You must have a backup policy defining what gets backed up, how often, how long backups are kept, and where they are stored. Backups must be encrypted, stored at a geographically separate site, and protected from both unauthorised access and ransomware.
evidence: Backup encryption and offsite storage evidence

artifacts.name: Backup Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining backup scope, frequency, retention, and storage requirements

artifacts.name: Backup Configuration Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of backup encryption, offsite storage, and ransomware protection

artifacts.name: Backup Execution Logs
artifacts.description: Logs of backup execution showing successful completion

title: Backup Testing & Restoration
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct regular backup restoration tests to verify the integrity and recoverability of backup data. Restoration tests shall be performed at least quarterly for critical systems and at least annually for non-critical systems. Test results shall be documented and failures remediated.
translation: Regularly test that your backups can actually be restored. For critical systems, test quarterly; for non-critical systems, test at least yearly. Document the results and fix any issues found.
evidence: Restoration test results for critical systems (quarterly)
Restoration test results for non-critical systems (annual)

artifacts.name: Backup Testing Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for regular backup restoration testing

artifacts.name: Backup Restoration Test Reports
artifacts.description: Reports on backup restoration test results and data integrity verification

artifacts.name: Test Failure Remediation Records
artifacts.description: Records of remediation actions for backup restoration test failures

title: Vendor Risk Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct comprehensive risk assessments of all technology vendors prior to engagement and on an ongoing basis. Assessments shall evaluate the vendor's security posture, financial stability, regulatory compliance, and business continuity capabilities. Critical vendor assessments shall be reviewed at least annually.
translation: Before engaging any technology vendor, and on an ongoing basis, conduct a thorough risk assessment covering their security, financial health, compliance, and continuity capabilities. For critical vendors, repeat this assessment at least yearly.
evidence: Vendor risk assessment framework and methodology
Vendor risk rating and classification records

artifacts.name: Vendor Risk Assessment Framework
artifacts.description: Framework and methodology for assessing technology vendor risks

artifacts.name: Vendor Risk Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Completed risk assessments for all technology vendors

artifacts.name: Annual Vendor Reassessment Records
artifacts.description: Records of annual risk reassessments for critical vendors

title: Outsourcing Governance
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a governance framework for technology outsourcing that defines roles, responsibilities, and oversight mechanisms. The framework shall ensure that outsourced activities are subject to the same risk management and security standards as in-house operations. Board approval shall be obtained for material outsourcing arrangements.
translation: You need a formal governance framework for technology outsourcing with clear roles and oversight. Outsourced activities must meet the same security and risk standards as internal operations. Material outsourcing arrangements need board approval.
evidence: Board approval records for material outsourcing

artifacts.name: Outsourcing Governance Framework
artifacts.description: Framework defining roles, responsibilities, and oversight for technology outsourcing

artifacts.name: Board Outsourcing Approvals
artifacts.description: Board approval records for material outsourcing arrangements

artifacts.name: Outsourcing Monitoring Reports
artifacts.description: Reports on oversight and monitoring of outsourced technology activities

title: SLA Monitoring
verbatim: Financial institutions shall monitor vendor compliance with service level agreements on an ongoing basis. SLA breaches shall be documented, escalated, and addressed through defined remediation processes. Persistent SLA non-compliance shall trigger vendor review and potential contract renegotiation or termination.
translation: Actively monitor whether your vendors are meeting their SLAs. When breaches occur, document them and escalate through defined processes. If a vendor consistently fails to meet SLAs, trigger a formal review that may lead to contract renegotiation or termination.
evidence: SLA breach records and escalation evidence

artifacts.name: Vendor SLA Monitoring Reports
artifacts.description: Regular reports on vendor SLA compliance and performance

artifacts.name: SLA Breach Records
artifacts.description: Records of SLA breaches with escalation and remediation actions

artifacts.name: Vendor Performance Reviews
artifacts.description: Formal vendor performance review records addressing SLA compliance

title: Vendor Exit Strategy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall maintain documented exit strategies for all critical technology vendors. Exit strategies shall address data migration, service transition, continuity of operations, and the secure destruction of data held by the vendor. Exit plans shall be tested or reviewed at least annually.
translation: For every critical vendor, you must have a documented exit plan covering how to migrate data, transition services, maintain operations during the switch, and ensure the vendor securely destroys your data. Review or test these plans at least yearly.
evidence: Annual exit plan review or test records

artifacts.name: Vendor Exit Strategy
artifacts.description: Documented exit strategies for all critical technology vendors

artifacts.name: Data Migration Plan
artifacts.description: Plan for migrating data and transitioning services from vendor

artifacts.name: Exit Plan Review Records
artifacts.description: Annual review or testing records for vendor exit strategies

title: Cloud Risk Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct a comprehensive risk assessment prior to adopting any cloud service. The assessment shall evaluate data sovereignty, regulatory compliance, security controls, vendor lock-in risks, and the shared responsibility model. Risk assessments shall be reviewed upon material changes to the cloud arrangement.
translation: Before adopting any cloud service, do a thorough risk assessment covering data sovereignty, compliance, security, vendor lock-in, and the shared responsibility model. Reassess whenever there are material changes to the cloud arrangement.
evidence: Risk assessment review records for material changes

artifacts.name: Cloud Risk Assessment Methodology
artifacts.description: Methodology for assessing risks of cloud service adoption

artifacts.name: Cloud Risk Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Completed risk assessments for all cloud service adoptions

artifacts.name: Shared Responsibility Documentation
artifacts.description: Documentation of shared responsibility model for each cloud provider

title: Access Control Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish, document, and maintain a formal access control policy that defines the rules and rights for users and groups with respect to all systems and information assets. The policy shall be reviewed at least annually or upon significant changes to the operating environment.
translation: You must have a written policy stating who can access what systems and data, what they are allowed to do, and how access is managed. This policy must be formally reviewed every year — or sooner if your environment changes significantly.
evidence: Access control policy document with version and effective date
Annual policy review record or sign-off
List of systems and information assets in scope

artifacts.name: Access Control Policy
artifacts.description: Formal access control policy defining rules and rights for all systems and assets

artifacts.name: Annual Policy Review Records
artifacts.description: Records of annual review and approval of the access control policy

artifacts.name: Policy Communication Records
artifacts.description: Evidence of access control policy communication to all staff

title: Cloud Data Residency
verbatim: Financial institutions shall ensure that data stored in cloud environments complies with applicable data residency and sovereignty requirements. Data classification shall determine the permissible locations for data storage and processing. The financial institution shall maintain visibility over the physical location of its data at all times.
translation: You must know exactly where your cloud data is stored and ensure it complies with data residency requirements. Data classification should drive decisions about where data can be stored and processed. You need full visibility over physical data locations at all times.

artifacts.name: Cloud Data Residency Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining data residency requirements for cloud-hosted data

artifacts.name: Data Location Mapping
artifacts.description: Mapping of data classifications to permissible storage and processing locations

artifacts.name: Data Residency Agreements
artifacts.description: Agreements with cloud providers confirming data residency commitments

title: User Access Provisioning
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement formal user access provisioning and de-provisioning processes that follow the principle of least privilege. Access shall be granted based on business need and job role, with formal approval from the data or system owner. De-provisioning shall occur within twenty-four hours of employment termination.
translation: User access must follow formal provisioning and de-provisioning processes based on least privilege. Access needs formal approval from the relevant system or data owner. When someone leaves, their access must be removed within 24 hours.

artifacts.name: User Access Provisioning Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for granting, modifying, and revoking user access based on least privilege

artifacts.name: Access Request and Approval Logs
artifacts.description: Logs of access requests with approvals from system or data owners

artifacts.name: De-provisioning Compliance Records
artifacts.description: Records showing access removal within 24 hours of termination

title: Multi-Factor Authentication
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all privileged access to critical systems, networks, and applications. The authentication mechanism must be resistant to replay attacks and phishing attempts. SMS one-time passwords (OTP) shall not be used for privileged access.
translation: Any administrator or privileged user accessing critical systems must use at least two forms of authentication — a password plus either a hardware token or an authenticator app (TOTP). SMS codes are explicitly not acceptable. The system must also prevent attackers from reusing stolen credentials.
evidence: Asset register confirming critical system scope
MFA configuration screenshots or audit logs

artifacts.name: MFA Policy
artifacts.description: Policy mandating multi-factor authentication for all privileged access to critical systems

artifacts.name: MFA Configuration Evidence
artifacts.description: Screenshots or audit logs demonstrating MFA configuration and enforcement

artifacts.name: Break-Glass Procedure
artifacts.description: Emergency access procedure with MFA controls for break-glass scenarios

artifacts.name: MFA Compliance Report
artifacts.description: Report on MFA adoption and compliance across critical systems

title: Password Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement a password policy that enforces minimum complexity requirements, password history, maximum password age, and account lockout thresholds. The policy shall mandate a minimum password length of twelve characters for standard users and sixteen characters for privileged accounts.
translation: You must have a password policy enforcing minimum length (12 characters for regular users, 16 for privileged accounts), complexity rules, password history (preventing reuse), maximum age, and account lockout after failed attempts.

artifacts.name: Password Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining password complexity, history, age, and lockout requirements

artifacts.name: Password Policy Configuration
artifacts.description: Technical configuration evidence showing password policy enforcement

artifacts.name: Password Policy Compliance Report
artifacts.description: Report on password policy compliance across all systems

title: Privileged Access Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement controls to manage, monitor, and review privileged access rights. All privileged activities shall be logged and the logs retained for a minimum of three years. Shared privileged accounts shall be eliminated except where operationally justified and subject to compensating controls.
translation: You need formal controls over who has admin-level access — a PAM system to manage and record privileged sessions, logs kept for at least 3 years, and a clear policy against shared admin accounts. If a shared account cannot be avoided, you need documented approval and compensating controls.

artifacts.name: PAM System Configuration
artifacts.description: Configuration evidence for privileged access management system

artifacts.name: Privileged Access Policy
artifacts.description: Policy governing privileged access management, logging, and shared account restrictions

artifacts.name: Privileged Activity Logs
artifacts.description: Logs of all privileged activities retained for a minimum of three years

artifacts.name: Shared Account Exception Register
artifacts.description: Register of approved shared privileged accounts with compensating controls

title: Access Review & Recertification
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct periodic access reviews and recertification for all systems and applications. Reviews shall be conducted at least quarterly for privileged access and at least semi-annually for standard user access. Access that is no longer required shall be promptly revoked.
translation: Regularly review and recertify all user access. Privileged access must be reviewed at least quarterly; standard user access at least every six months. Any access that is no longer justified must be revoked promptly.

artifacts.name: Access Review Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for periodic access reviews and recertification

artifacts.name: Privileged Access Review Records
artifacts.description: Quarterly review records for all privileged access

artifacts.name: Standard Access Review Records
artifacts.description: Semi-annual review records for standard user access

artifacts.description: Evidence of access revocation resulting from review findings
